1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.
12	
13	## Skills
14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs (OpenAI/Gemini/OpenRouter) for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. **Requires `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` in env** — check via `env | grep -E "OPENAI|GEMINI|OPENROUTER"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)
47	
48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All Archon state files are in `.archon/`. The table below covers **phase agents** (plan, prover, review) and the **user**. Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row — they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See "Subagent permissions" after the table.
51	
52	| File | Plan | Prover | Review | User |
53	|------|------|--------|--------|------|
54	| `PROGRESS.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
55	| `STRATEGY.md` | read + write | — | — | read |
56	| `USER_HINTS.md` | **loop-managed** (captured + cleared by the CLI; injected into the plan prompt) | — | — | write |
57	| `task_pending.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
58	| `task_done.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
59	| `task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect) | write (own file) | read only | read |
60	| `proof-journal/` | read | — | **write** | read |
61	| `PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | — | **write** (Knowledge Base only — session log lives in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`) | read |
62	| `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` | **write** (this iter only) | — | read (recent window injected) | read |
63	| `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (recent window injected) | — | **write** (this iter only) | read |
64	| `iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md` | optional write | — | read | read |
65	| `archon-protected.yaml` | read | read | read | **write** |
66	| `.lean` files | — | write (own file; frozen protected signatures) | — | write (via comments) |
67	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints) | — | **write** (semantic markers: `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections — NOT `\leanok`) | read |
68	| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | — | — | read |
69	| `TO_USER.md` | — (review owns) | — | **write** (reset at review start) | read |
70	
71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.
82	
83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	## Agent Roles
112	
113	### Plan Agent
114	
115	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
116	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
117	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
118	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
119	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
120	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
121	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
122	
123	### Prover Agent
124	
125	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
126	- Read the stage-specific prompt from `.archon/prompts/` (`prover-autoformalize.md` / `prover-prover.md` / `prover-polish.md`).
127	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
128	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
129	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
130	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
131	
132	### Subagents
133	
134	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
135	
136	### Review Agent
137	
138	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
139	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
140	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
141	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
142	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
143	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
144	
145	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
146	
147	These run automatically between phases:
148	
149	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
150	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
151	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.

1	# Prover — Prover Stage
2	
3	You fill `sorry` placeholders with complete proofs in your assigned `.lean` file.
4	
5	## Workflow
6	
7	1. Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
8	2. Read `task_pending.md` for prior attempts, dead ends, relevant lemmas on your file.
9	3. Check the `.lean` file for `/- USER: ... -/` comments (file-specific user hints).
10	4. **Read the relevant blueprint chapter before writing Lean code.** The chapter holds the mathematical proof sketch you must align with. When stuck, re-reading the blueprint is often the fastest path forward.
11	5. Replace `sorry` with Lean proofs. Push as far as possible.
12	6. **Always save partial progress in the code.** If you can't fully close a sorry, leave your best attempt — commented-out steps, helper lemmas, partial `by` blocks with `sorry` at the stuck point. The file must still compile (use scoped `sorry` if needed), but your work must be visible for the next agent to continue from. NEVER revert to a bare `sorry` — that wastes the work.
13	7. Write your results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
14	
15	**Write permissions**: only your assigned `.lean` file(s) and `task_results/<your_file>.md`. Do NOT edit `PROGRESS.md`, `task_pending.md`, `task_done.md`, blueprint chapters, or other agents' files.
16	
17	## Protected declarations
18	
19	`archon-protected.yaml` lists declarations with **frozen signatures**. You may fill their proof bodies but must not rename, re-type, reorder arguments, or weaken hypotheses. Only the mathematician edits protected signatures.
20	
21	## Avoid early termination
22	
23	- Don't abandon a proof prematurely. Many complex proofs run to thousands of lines.
24	- Difficulty is NOT a valid reason to leave a `sorry`.
25	- Don't delegate to "the next iteration" or "another prover" if more effort could close it.
26	- Only modify the proof for your assigned task — leave unrelated proofs untouched.
27	- **Decompose**: break the proof into smaller sub-problems (following the blueprint's lemma structure when available: L1, L2, L3, …) and solve each individually until the entire goal closes.
28	
29	## Task completion criteria
30	
31	Your task is complete ONLY when ALL of:
32	
33	1. Every `sorry` in scope is replaced with a complete proof.
34	2. Zero axioms introduced.
35	3. The file compiles cleanly.
36	
37	## Never weaken the type to dodge the proof
38	
39	When the substantive type is unattainable this iter, you have one honest move: leave `sorry` with the **intended type signature** so the type itself encodes the claim. You must NOT substitute a tautologically-true type to trivialize the body. Three patterns are banned:
40	
41	- **Reflexive-iso placeholder.** Replacing a non-trivial `X ≅ Y` (or `Nonempty (X ≅ Y)`) target with `Nonempty (X ≅ X) := ⟨Iso.refl _⟩`. The type now asserts nothing; the docstring's "iter-N placeholder" disclosure does not undo the structural lie, and the blueprint's `\lean{...}` cross-reference will resolve to this hollow declaration.
42	- **`Classical.choice` body around an explicit witness.** Writing `Classical.choice (α := X) ⟨witness⟩` to dissolve `Type` vs `Prop` friction — the witness is unreachable through any unfold, so the def is indistinguishable from `Classical.choice ⟨any-other-X⟩` downstream. Either use `Classical.choose` on the existential chain (preserves the spec), or `Nonempty.some`-with-witness; or ship a typed `sorry`.
43	- **Empty-content `proof_wanted`.** `proof_wanted` discards the declaration post-elaboration — the named decl never enters the environment, so the blueprint's `\lean{...}` cross-reference breaks. Use `theorem name : T := by sorry` with the intended signature instead.
44	
45	**Litmus test**: if you `unfold` your declaration, does it expose the named substantive content (Kähler differential module, the explicit iso, …) or does it stop at `Classical.choice` / `Iso.refl _` / nothing? If the latter, the body is structurally vacuous — ship the typed `sorry` instead.
46	
47	## When infrastructure is missing
48	
49	Do NOT report "Mathlib lacks X" and stop. Before giving up:
50	
51	1. **Use the informal agent** (`.claude/tools/archon-informal-agent.py`): "Prove [goal] without using [missing infrastructure], only Mathlib." Even an imperfect sketch is valuable.
52	2. **Try the alternative** — formalize whatever the informal agent suggests.
53	3. **If you still can't**: write the alternative sketch to `informal/<theorem_name>.md` and record in your task result what you tried, why it failed, AND the alternative route you found. "I couldn't prove X, but here's approach Y that might work because Z" is far more useful than "infrastructure missing".
54	
55	When stuck more generally: break into smaller subgoals, search Mathlib more thoroughly, prove missing helpers yourself, try alternative strategies, re-read the blueprint, use Web Search for published proofs.
56	
57	**Impossibility vs difficulty**: technical difficulty → keep trying. Mathematical impossibility → immediately backtrack and document why.
58	
59	## Proof style
60	
61	- **Never modify working proofs** — if a declaration has no `sorry` and compiles, do not touch its body.
62	- Keep edits minimal; don't delete comments or change labels; don't add unrelated declarations.
63	- Helper lemmas you introduced may be modified if they turn out wrong.
64	- Add a concise comment above each helper lemma so reuse is easy.
65	- **`change` vs `show`** — `change` reshapes the goal up to defeq; `show` is purely display-level annotation. Using `show` where `change` is needed produces a linter warning. Default to `change` when in doubt.
66	
67	## Mathlib tags in PROGRESS.md
68	
69	The plan agent tags suggested lemmas:
70	
71	- `[verified]` — confirmed to exist.
72	- `[expected]` — guessed by naming convention. Quick `lean_local_search`; pivot if it doesn't exist.
73	- `[gap]` — verified NOT in Mathlib. Don't waste search time; formalize a workaround.
74	
75	## LSP MCP tools
76	
77	The `archon-lean-lsp` server exposes Lean LSP operations as **MCP tool calls** (full names like `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_goal` / `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_diagnostic_messages`). The lean4 skill reference uses short names (`lean_goal`, `lean_local_search`, …) — same tools, NOT shell binaries.
78	
79	- Always invoke through your tool-call interface, the same way as `Read` or `Grep`.
80	- **Never** call `Bash` with `lean_goal …` etc. — there is no such shell command.
81	- First LSP action: `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_diagnostic_messages` on your file. If it returns `success: false` the server is cold; retry once or run `lake build` once via Bash, then retry.
82	
83	## Search protocol
84	
85	Follow `references/lean-lsp-tools-api.md`. Key priority:
86	
87	1. `lean_local_search` first.
88	2. `lean_leansearch` for semantic search — describe the mathematical content, not just the name.
89	3. `lean_loogle` for simple type patterns only.
90	4. Never use shell `find` / `grep` to locate Mathlib theorems.
91	
92	## Tooling traps
93	
94	- **Trust `goals_after`, not just empty diagnostics**: in `lean_multi_attempt`, `diagnostics: []` can be misleading (line-scoping quirks attach errors to the enclosing `by` block). Check that `goals_after` is empty or has advanced.
95	- **Beware `lean_run_code` with imports**: standalone snippets can silently swallow elaboration errors when they contain imports. Rely on the actual file's `lean_diagnostic_messages` for authoritative verification.
96	
97	## Logging
98	
99	Write to `task_results/<your_file>.md` (mirror your `.lean` path: `Algebra/WLocal.lean` → `task_results/Algebra_WLocal.lean.md`):
100	
101	```markdown
102	# Algebra/WLocal.lean
103	
104	## wLocal_iff (line 45)
105	### Attempt 1
106	- **Approach:** Direct case split on maximal ideals
107	- **Result:** FAILED — needed IsLocalRing instance, not available
108	- **Dead end:** direct case split without IsLocalRing
109	
110	### Attempt 2
111	- **Approach:** Stacks 0A31, characterize via bijection on spectra
112	- **Result:** RESOLVED
113	- **Key insight:** `PrimeSpectrum.comap_injective` bridges the gap
114	
115	## helper_bijective (line 78)
116	### Attempt 1
117	- **Approach:** Split into injectivity + surjectivity; prove injectivity
118	- **Result:** PARTIAL (branch closed) — injectivity closed; surjectivity remains
119	- **Next step:** `PrimeSpectrum.range_comap_of_surjective` for the right branch
120	- **Lemmas found:** `PrimeSpectrum.comap_surjective`
121	```
122	
123	**Rules**: one section per theorem/lemma in your file. Each attempt records approach, result (RESOLVED / FAILED / PARTIAL / IN PROGRESS), dead-end warnings or next steps. Log negative search results ("Searched 'projective module infinite rank' — nothing in Mathlib"). The plan agent merges these into `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md` next iter.
124	
125	**Read-only context**: read `task_pending.md` (prior attempts on your file) and `task_done.md` (when the problem resembles a completed one). Do not write to either.
126	
127	## End-of-session handoff
128

10	/-!
11	# The Riemann–Roch formula in genus zero (RR.2)
12	
13	This file is the **RR.2** file-skeleton sub-build chapter for the project's
14	headline `genusZero_curve_iso_P1` (the "smooth proper geometrically
15	irreducible genus-`0` curve over `k̄` is isomorphic to `ℙ¹`" lemma in
16	`AlgebraicJacobian.AbelianVarietyRigidity`).
17	
18	The Hartshorne IV.1.3.5 chain for the genus-`0` ↦ `ℙ¹` classification routes
19	through:
20	
21	- `RR.1` (`RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor.tex` / `WeilDivisor.lean`): the Weil
22	  divisor group `Div(C)` and the degree map `deg : Div(C) → ℤ`.
23	- **`RR.2` (this file, `RiemannRoch_RRFormula.tex`)**: the Riemann–Roch
24	  dimension formula `ℓ(D) = deg(D) + 1` in genus `0` (with `deg D ≥ 0`),
25	  via the auxiliary Euler-characteristic identity
26	  `χ(𝒪_C(D)) = deg(D) + 1 − g`.
27	- `RR.3` (`RiemannRoch_OcOfD.tex`, future): the invertible sheaf
28	  `𝒪_C(D)`, the linear-equivalence isomorphism `𝒪_C(D) ≅ 𝒪_C(D')` for
29	  `D ∼ D'`, and the `H¹`-vanishing input
30	  `H¹(C, 𝒪_C(D)) = 0` for `deg D ≥ 0` on a genus-`0` curve.
31	- `RR.4` (`RiemannRoch_RationalIsoP1.tex`, future): the "two-section
32	  ⇒ `Proj.fromOfGlobalSections` ⇒ `≅ ℙ¹`" classification.
33	
34	## Status (iter-174 Lane F file-skeleton)
35	
36	This file is the **iter-174 Lane F** file-skeleton: each of the four pinned
37	declarations carries the *intended* substantive type signature (matching the
38	blueprint `\lean{...}` pin in `chapters/RiemannRoch_RRFormula.tex`). The
39	Euler-characteristic carrier definition is concrete (a one-line subtraction
40	of `H⁰` and `H¹` `Module.finrank`s, mirroring the `genus` definition of
41	`AlgebraicJacobian.Genus`); the remaining pins carry `sorry` bodies whose
42	closure is iter-175+ work after the sibling chapters `RR.3`
43	(`RiemannRoch_OcOfD.tex`) and `RR.4` (`RiemannRoch_RationalIsoP1.tex`) land.
44	
45	The 4 pinned declarations are:
46	
47	1. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.eulerCharacteristic` — Euler characteristic
48	   `χ(𝓕) = dim_{k̄} H⁰(C, 𝓕) − dim_{k̄} H¹(C, 𝓕)` of a `ModuleCat k̄`-valued
49	   sheaf on `C` (the curve specialisation of the alternating sum, since
50	   `H^i = 0` for `i ≥ 2` on a one-dimensional scheme by Grothendieck
51	   vanishing).
52	2. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.l` — the `ℓ`-invariant
53	   `ℓ(D) = dim_{k̄} H⁰(C, 𝒪_C(D))` of a Weil divisor `D`.
54	3. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.eulerCharacteristic_eq_degree_plus_one_minus_genus`
55	   — the Euler-characteristic identity `χ(𝒪_C(D)) = deg(D) + 1 − g` for
56	   every `D ∈ Div(C)` on a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve
57	   `C / k̄` of genus `g = g(C)`.
58	4. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.l_eq_degree_plus_one_of_genus_zero`
59	   — the Riemann–Roch formula in genus `0`: `ℓ(D) = deg(D) + 1` for any
60	   Weil divisor `D ∈ Div(C)` with `deg D ≥ 0` on a smooth proper
61	   geometrically irreducible curve `C / k̄` with `g(C) = 0` (threading
62	   the `H¹`-vanishing of `𝒪_C(D)` explicitly as a named premise until
63	   `RR.3` lands).
64	
65	## Note on `𝒪_C(D)` (the invertible sheaf of a divisor)
66	
67	The chapter's proof of `eulerCharacteristic_eq_degree_plus_one_minus_genus`
68	and the statement of `l_eq_degree_plus_one_of_genus_zero` both reference
69	the line bundle `𝒪_C(D)` of a Weil divisor `D`. Mathlib `b80f227` ships no
70	`Scheme.lineBundleOfDivisor` (the closest is `WeierstrassCurve.lineBundle`
71	in the elliptic-curve formalisation), and the project-side construction of
72	`𝒪_C(D)` is queued for `RR.3` (`RiemannRoch_OcOfD.tex`). To keep the type
73	signatures of pins 2–4 substantive in the iter-174 skeleton, we expose a
74	**typed-`sorry` placeholder**
75	`AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.sheafOf` that pairs each divisor with
76	the `ModuleCat k̄`-valued sheaf carrier `𝒪_C(D)` is intended to occupy. The
77	iter-175+ closure of `RR.3` replaces this placeholder's body with the
78	honest invertible-sheaf construction; the present pins consume it only
79	through its `H⁰` and `H¹` cohomology, so the consumer signatures are
80	substantive in the type sense (each asserts an arithmetic identity on the
81	finiteness-of-`H^*` outputs).

100	/-! ## §1. The Euler characteristic of a coherent sheaf on a curve -/
101	
102	/-- **Euler characteristic of a `ModuleCat k`-valued sheaf on a smooth proper
103	curve `C / k̄`.**
104	
105	On a curve (one-dimensional scheme), Grothendieck vanishing
106	(Hartshorne III.2.7) gives `H^i(C, 𝓕) = 0` for `i ≥ 2`, so the classical
107	alternating sum
108	`χ(𝓕) = Σ_{i ≥ 0} (-1)^i dim_{k̄} H^i(C, 𝓕)` collapses to the two-term
109	expression
110	`χ(𝓕) = dim_{k̄} H⁰(C, 𝓕) − dim_{k̄} H¹(C, 𝓕)`.
111	
112	This is the definition we ship. Coherence of `𝓕` on the proper `k̄`-scheme
113	`C` guarantees that both `H⁰` and `H¹` are finite-dimensional `k̄`-vector
114	spaces (Serre's coherent-cohomology finiteness theorem, the same
115	finiteness backing `AlgebraicGeometry.genus`), so the two `Module.finrank`s
116	are honest natural numbers and the difference is a well-defined integer.
117	
118	The `Module k̄`-valued cohomology pipeline is the project's
119	`Scheme.HModule` (iter-009), the same wrapper used by
120	`AlgebraicGeometry.genus`.
121	
122	Blueprint reference: `def:eulerChar_curve`
123	(Hartshorne IV.1 p. 295, displayed inside the proof of Theorem 1.3). -/
124	noncomputable def Scheme.eulerCharacteristic
125	    {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]
126	    (C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))) [IsProper C.hom]
127	    [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]
128	    [GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom]
129	    (F : Sheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology C.left.toTopCat)
130	      (ModuleCat.{u} kbar)) : ℤ :=
131	  (Module.finrank kbar (Scheme.HModule kbar F 0) : ℤ)
132	    - (Module.finrank kbar (Scheme.HModule kbar F 1) : ℤ)
133	
134	/-! ## §2. The invertible sheaf `𝒪_C(D)` of a Weil divisor (placeholder)
135	
136	The honest construction of `𝒪_C(D)` lives in the sibling chapter `RR.3`
137	(`RiemannRoch_OcOfD.tex`), where the locally-principal ideal sheaves of
138	closed points are glued into an invertible `𝒪_C`-module. To keep the
139	iter-174 skeleton's pin signatures substantive, we expose `𝒪_C(D)` as a
140	typed-`sorry` `ModuleCat k̄`-valued sheaf-on-`C`. The iter-175+ closure of
141	`RR.3` will replace the body with the construction; downstream pins (the
142	`ℓ`-invariant, the χ-identity, the genus-`0` Riemann–Roch formula) consume
143	this placeholder only through its `H⁰` and `H¹`, so their signatures
144	encode arithmetic identities on finiteness-of-cohomology outputs and are
145	not vacuous. -/
146	
147	namespace Scheme.WeilDivisor
148	
149	variable {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]
150	  {C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))} [IsProper C.hom]
151	  [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]
152	  [GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom] [IsIntegral C.left]
153	
154	/-- **The invertible sheaf `𝒪_C(D)` of a Weil divisor `D` on a smooth proper
155	curve `C / k̄`** (placeholder for `RR.3`).
156	
157	For a Weil divisor `D = Σᵢ nᵢ · [Pᵢ]` on a smooth proper geometrically
158	irreducible curve `C / k̄`, the associated invertible sheaf `𝒪_C(D)` is the
159	locally-free `𝒪_C`-module of rank one cut out (locally near each `Pᵢ`) by
160	the `(−nᵢ)`-th power of the local uniformiser at `Pᵢ`. Hartshorne II §6
161	Proposition 6.13 packages this construction; the project-side `RR.3`
162	chapter (`RiemannRoch_OcOfD.tex`, to be added) will produce the carrier as
163	a `Sheaf` of `ModuleCat k̄`-modules on `C.left`.
164	
165	In the iter-174 skeleton this is a typed-`sorry`. The body in `RR.3` will
166	be the invertible-sheaf glue of the locally principal ideals of the
167	support of `D`. -/
168	noncomputable def sheafOf (_D : C.left.WeilDivisor) :
169	    Sheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology C.left.toTopCat)
170	      (ModuleCat.{u} kbar) :=
171	  sorry
172	
173	/-! ## §3. The `ℓ`-invariant of a Weil divisor -/
174	
175	/-- **The `ℓ`-invariant of a Weil divisor `D` on a smooth proper curve
176	`C / k̄`.**
177	
178	By definition,
179	`ℓ(D) := dim_{k̄} H⁰(C, 𝒪_C(D)) ∈ ℕ`,
180	the `k̄`-dimension of the space of global sections of the invertible sheaf
181	`𝒪_C(D)` associated to `D`. Finiteness of `ℓ(D)` is a consequence of the
182	coherent-cohomology finiteness theorem on a proper `k̄`-scheme
183	(Hartshorne II.5.19 / III.5.2), the same input that backs the finiteness
184	of `genus C`.
185	
186	The classical *complete linear system* `|D|` has `k̄`-projective dimension
187	`ℓ(D) − 1`; the chapter never uses this projective interpretation —
188	`ℓ(D)` is the only quantity consumed by the Hartshorne IV.1.3.5 chain.
189	
190	Blueprint reference: `def:l_invariant` (Hartshorne IV.1 p. 295,
191	"We denote `dim_k H⁰(X, 𝒪(D))` by `l(D)`"). -/
192	noncomputable def l (D : C.left.WeilDivisor) : ℕ :=
193	  Module.finrank kbar (Scheme.HModule kbar (sheafOf (C := C) D) 0)
194	
195	end Scheme.WeilDivisor
196	
197	/-! ## §4. The χ-identity: `χ(𝒪_C(D)) = deg(D) + 1 − g`
198	
199	The bridge from the structural Euler characteristic to the arithmetic
200	degree of a divisor. Hartshorne IV.1 Theorem 1.3 reduces Riemann–Roch to
201	this identity; the proof is the inductive `D ↔ D + [P]` step (additivity
202	of χ on the closed-point short exact sequence, with base case `D = 0`
203	giving `χ(𝒪_C) = 1 − g` from `dim H⁰(C, 𝒪_C) = 1` and the definition of
204	the genus). The closure of the body is iter-175+ work after the
205	`Euler-characteristic additivity on a short exact sequence`-style
206	project-side helper is supplied and the `RR.3` sheaf `𝒪_C(D)` has a
207	body. -/
208	
209	/-- **Euler-characteristic identity for `𝒪_C(D)` on a smooth proper curve
210	of genus `g`.**
211	
212	For every Weil divisor `D ∈ Div(C)`,
213	`χ(𝒪_C(D)) = deg(D) + 1 − g(C)`.
214	
215	The proof is Hartshorne IV.1 Theorem 1.3's reduction: induction on the
216	free-abelian-group structure of `Div(C)` on closed points, base case `D =
217	0` giving `χ(𝒪_C) = 1 − g` (the `dim H⁰(C, 𝒪_C) = 1` is the Hartshorne
218	I.3.4 input via the project's `H⁰`-bridge), and inductive step via
219	additivity of χ on the closed-point short exact sequence
220	`0 → 𝒪_C(D) → 𝒪_C(D + [P]) → k(P) → 0`.
221	
222	Blueprint reference: `thm:euler_char_eq_deg_plus_one_minus_genus`
223	(Hartshorne IV.1 Theorem 1.3, p. 295). -/
224	theorem Scheme.eulerCharacteristic_eq_degree_plus_one_minus_genus
225	    {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]
226	    (C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))) [IsProper C.hom]
227	    [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]
228	    [GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom] [IsIntegral C.left]
229	    (D : C.left.WeilDivisor) :
230	    Scheme.eulerCharacteristic C (Scheme.WeilDivisor.sheafOf (C := C) D)
231	      = (Scheme.WeilDivisor.degree D) + 1 - (AlgebraicGeometry.genus C : ℤ) :=
232	  sorry
233	
234	/-! ## §5. The Riemann–Roch formula in genus zero -/
235	
236	/-- **Riemann–Roch in genus zero (Hartshorne IV.1 Example 1.3.5).**
237	
238	Let `C` be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over the
239	algebraically closed field `k̄` with `g(C) = 0`, and let `D ∈ Div(C)` with
240	`deg D ≥ 0`. Then
241	`ℓ(D) = deg(D) + 1`.
242	
243	The proof specialises the χ-identity
244	`eulerCharacteristic_eq_degree_plus_one_minus_genus` to `g = 0`,
245	unfolds `χ` via `def:eulerChar_curve`, and absorbs the `H¹`-vanishing
246	hypothesis (named premise `_hH1`: `H¹(C, 𝒪_C(D)) = 0`, which is the
247	`H¹`-vanishing of a non-negative-degree invertible sheaf on a
248	genus-`0` curve, to be discharged by `RR.3` once `𝒪_C(D)` has a body and
249	the cohomology of `𝒪_{ℙ¹}(d)` is computed).
250	
251	Blueprint reference: `thm:riemannRoch_genus_zero` (Hartshorne IV.1
252	Example 1.3.5, p. 297). -/
253	theorem Scheme.WeilDivisor.l_eq_degree_plus_one_of_genus_zero
254	    {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]
255	    (C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))) [IsProper C.hom]
256	    [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]
257	    [GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom] [IsIntegral C.left]
258	    (D : C.left.WeilDivisor) (_hg : AlgebraicGeometry.genus C = 0)
259	    (_hdeg : (0 : ℤ) ≤ Scheme.WeilDivisor.degree D)
260	    (_hH1 : Module.finrank kbar
261	      (Scheme.HModule kbar (Scheme.WeilDivisor.sheafOf (C := C) D) 1) = 0) :
262	    (Scheme.WeilDivisor.l (C := C) D : ℤ)
263	      = Scheme.WeilDivisor.degree D + 1 :=
264	  sorry
265	
266	end AlgebraicGeometry

3	\chapter{The Riemann--Roch formula in genus zero (RR.2)}
4	\label{chap:RiemannRoch_RRFormula}
5	
6	% SOURCE: Hartshorne, \emph{Algebraic Geometry}, IV \S1, pp.~294--297
7	% (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF pages
8	% 311--314). The verbatim quotes pasted in the declaration blocks below
9	% were copied character-by-character from the rendered page images of
10	% the scanned PDF (no text layer is present).
11	
12	\section{Setup and motivation}
13	
14	This chapter is the second of four sub-build chapters \texttt{RR.1}--\texttt{RR.4}
15	that close the project's headline RR bridge
16	\[
17	  \mathtt{genusZero\_curve\_iso\_P1}
18	  \,\colon\,
19	  \text{a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve $C / \bar k$ with
20	  $g(C) = 0$ is isomorphic to $\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$.}
21	\]
22	The bridge consumed by
23	\Cref{prop:genusZero_curve_iso_P1} of
24	\texttt{AbelianVarietyRigidity.tex} is the Hartshorne~IV.1.3.5 chain:
25	the Weil-divisor group $\Div(C)$ and the degree map $\deg \colon \Div(C)
26	\to \mathbb Z$ (\texttt{RR.1}, \Cref{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor}); the
27	Riemann--Roch dimension formula in genus~$0$
28	\[
29	  \ell(D) \;=\; \deg(D) + 1 \qquad \text{for } \deg(D) \geq 0
30	\]
31	(this chapter, \texttt{RR.2}); the global-sections identification
32	$\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2$ (\texttt{RR.3}); the
33	``rational curve $\Rightarrow \cong \mathbb P^1$'' classification via
34	\texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections} (\texttt{RR.4}).
35	
36	% NOTE (Serre duality vs. direct $\chi$ — iter-173 sub-phase choice).
37	% This chapter proves the RR formula via the \emph{direct $\chi$
38	% inductive proof} (Hartshorne IV.1.3 step ``Next, let $D$ be any
39	% divisor\ldots'', p.~296), \emph{not} via Serre duality. The reason is
40	% strictly Mathlib-side: Mathlib (snapshot \texttt{b80f227}) ships no
41	% dualizing sheaf $\omega_X$ on a scheme and no Serre duality theorem
42	% $H^i(X, \mathcal F) \cong H^{n-i}(X, \omega_X \otimes \mathcal F^\vee)^*$;
43	% routing through that machinery would force a multi-iter sub-build
44	% strictly heavier than the genus-$0$ critical path requires. The
45	% direct $\chi$ proof depends only on additivity of the Euler
46	% characteristic on a short exact sequence of coherent sheaves (a
47	% standard preadditive-category fact), the genus base case
48	% $\chi(\mathcal O_C) = 1 - g$, and the closed-point skyscraper
49	% identity $\chi(k(P)) = 1$.
50	
51	% NOTE (Genus-$0$ carve-out vs. general $g$ — iter-173 sub-phase
52	% choice). The chapter restricts the headline theorem
53	% \Cref{thm:riemannRoch_genus_zero} to the genus-$0$ specialisation
54	% $\ell(D) = \deg(D) + 1$ for $\deg(D) \geq 0$. The general-$g$
55	% statement $\ell(D) - \ell(K - D) = \deg(D) + 1 - g$ is downstream of
56	% \texttt{RR.2} (it requires the canonical divisor $K$ and Serre
57	% duality / the $K - D$ pairing) and is \emph{not} needed for the
58	% genus-$0$ critical path: \texttt{RR.3} consumes only
59	% \Cref{thm:riemannRoch_genus_zero}, and \texttt{RR.4} consumes
60	% \texttt{RR.3}. The intermediate $\chi$-identity
61	% \Cref{thm:euler_char_eq_deg_plus_one_minus_genus} is stated and
62	% sketched for general $g$ (the inductive argument is genus-uniform);
63	% its full closure on a general-$g$ curve waits for the project's
64	% subsequent Riemann--Roch chapter, but the proof body in
65	% \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/RRFormula.lean} pins the
66	% \emph{statement} for general $g$ and may close the body initially
67	% under a $g = 0$ hypothesis.
68	
69	\paragraph{Standing hypotheses.} Throughout this chapter, $\bar k$ is an
70	algebraically closed field and $C$ is a smooth proper geometrically
71	irreducible curve over $\bar k$. Concretely, the Lean signatures
72	(\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/RRFormula.lean}) carry the same
73	typeclass package as the rest of the project's curve API:
74	\begin{quote}
75	  \texttt{\{kbar : Type u\} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]\\
76	   (C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))) [IsProper C.hom]\\
77	   \phantom{}\quad[SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]\\
78	   \phantom{}\quad[GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom] [IsIntegral C.left]}
79	\end{quote}
80	The genus $g(C)$ is the one defined in \Cref{def:genus} of
81	\texttt{Genus.tex}, namely $g(C) = \dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)$
82	via the project's $\Module\bar k$-flavoured sheaf cohomology
83	(\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.genus}).
84	
85	\section{The Euler characteristic of a coherent sheaf on a curve}
86	
87	\begin{definition}
88	
89	
90	\leanok
91	[Euler characteristic of a coherent sheaf on a curve]
92	  \label{def:eulerChar_curve}
93	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.eulerCharacteristic}
94	  \uses{def:genus}
95	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, p.~295 (definition of $\chi(\mathcal F)$
96	  % inside the proof of Theorem~1.3) (read from
97	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 312).
98	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Thus we have to show that for any $D$,
99	  % $\chi(\mathscr L(D)) = \deg D + 1 - g$,
100	  % where for any coherent sheaf $\mathscr F$ on $X$, $\chi(\mathscr F)$
101	  % is the Euler characteristic
102	  % $\chi(\mathscr F) = \dim H^0(X, \mathscr F) - \dim H^1(X, \mathscr F)$."
103	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, p.~295, the formula displayed
104	  inside the proof of Theorem~1.3.}
105	  Let $C$ be a smooth proper curve over $\bar k$ and let $\mathcal F$
106	  be a coherent sheaf on $C$. The \emph{Euler characteristic} of
107	  $\mathcal F$ is the integer
108	  \[
109	    \chi(\mathcal F) \;:=\;
110	    \sum_{i \geq 0} (-1)^i \,\dim_{\bar k} H^i(C, \mathcal F)
111	    \;=\;
112	    \dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal F)
113	    \,-\,
114	    \dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal F).
115	  \]
116	  The second equality is a curve-specific simplification: because
117	  $\dim C = 1$, Grothendieck vanishing (Hartshorne~III.2.7) gives
118	  $H^i(C, \mathcal F) = 0$ for $i \geq 2$, so the alternating sum has
119	  only two terms. Coherence of $\mathcal F$ on the proper $\bar
120	  k$-scheme $C$ guarantees that both $H^0$ and $H^1$ are
121	  finite-dimensional $\bar k$-vector spaces (Serre's coherent-cohomology
122	  finiteness theorem, the same finiteness backing
123	  \Cref{def:genus}), so the two terms are honest natural numbers and
124	  the difference is a well-defined integer.
125

140	\begin{definition}

153	  % $l(D)$ is finite by (II, 5.19) or (III, 5.2)."

156	  $D \in \Div(C)$ be a Weil divisor (\Cref{def:codim1_cycles}). The

158	  \[

161	  \]

167	  $\bar k$-dimension $\ell(D) - 1$ as a projective space, but the
168	  chapter never uses the projective-space interpretation; $\ell(D)$ is
169	  the only quantity it needs.

183	\section{The $\chi$ identity: $\chi(\mathcal O_C(D)) = \deg(D) + 1 - g$}
184	
185	\begin{theorem}
186	
187	
188	\leanok
189	[Euler-characteristic identity for $\mathcal O_C(D)$]
190	  \label{thm:euler_char_eq_deg_plus_one_minus_genus}
191	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.eulerCharacteristic_eq_degree_plus_one_minus_genus}
192	  \uses{def:eulerChar_curve, def:divisor_degree, def:genus,
193	        thm:divisor_degree_hom}
194	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, Theorem~1.3 (the reduction inside the
195	  % proof: ``Thus we have to show that for any $D$, $\chi(\mathscr L(D))
196	  % = \deg D + 1 - g$'') (read from
197	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 312).
198	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Theorem 1.3 (Riemann--Roch). \emph{Let $D$ be a
199	  % divisor on a curve $X$ of genus $g$. Then}
200	  % $l(D) - l(K - D) = \deg D + 1 - g$."
201	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Thus we have to show that for any $D$,
202	  % $\chi(\mathscr L(D)) = \deg D + 1 - g$,
203	  % where for any coherent sheaf $\mathscr F$ on $X$, $\chi(\mathscr F)$
204	  % is the Euler characteristic
205	  % $\chi(\mathscr F) = \dim H^0(X, \mathscr F) - \dim H^1(X, \mathscr F)$."
206	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, Theorem~1.3, p.~295.}
207	  Let $C$ be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
208	  $\bar k$ of genus $g = g(C)$, and let $D \in \Div(C)$. Then
209	  \[
210	    \chi(\mathcal O_C(D)) \;=\; \deg(D) + 1 - g.
211	  \]
212	\end{theorem}
213	
214	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "First we consider the case $D = 0$. Then our
215	% formula says
216	% $\dim H^0(X, \mathscr O_X) - \dim H^1(X, \mathscr O_X) = 1 - g$,
217	% because $H^0(X, \mathscr O_X) = k$ for any projective variety
218	% (I, 3.4), and $\dim H^1(X, \mathscr O_X) = g$ by (1.1).
219	% Next, let $D$ be any divisor, and let $P$ be any point. We will show
220	% that the formula is true for $D$ if and only if it is true for
221	% $D + P$. Since any divisor can be reached from $0$ in a finite number
222	% of steps by adding or subtracting a point each time, this will show
223	% the result holds for all $D$.
224	% We consider $P$ as a closed subscheme of $X$. Its structure sheaf is
225	% the skyscraper sheaf $k$ sitting at the point $P$, which we denote by
226	% $k(P)$, and its ideal sheaf is $\mathscr L(-P)$ by (II, 6.18).
227	% Therefore we have an exact sequence
228	% $0 \to \mathscr L(-P) \to \mathscr O_X \to k(P) \to 0$.
229	% Tensoring with $\mathscr L(D + P)$ we get
230	% $0 \to \mathscr L(D) \to \mathscr L(D + P) \to k(P) \to 0$,
231	% (since $\mathscr L(P)$ is locally free of rank 1, tensoring by it
232	% does not affect the sheaf $k(P)$). Now the Euler characteristic is
233	% additive on short exact sequences (III, Ex. 5.1), and $\chi(k(P))
234	% = 1$, so we have
235	% $\chi(\mathscr L(D + P)) = \chi(\mathscr L(D)) + 1$.
236	% On the other hand, $\deg D + P = \deg D + 1$, so our formula is true
237	% for $D$ if and only if it is true for $D + P$, as required."
238	\begin{proof}
239	  \uses{def:eulerChar_curve, def:divisor_degree, def:genus,
240	        thm:divisor_degree_hom, def:divisor_closed_point}
241	  The proof is by induction on the structure of $\Div(C)$ as the free
242	  abelian group on closed points (\Cref{def:codim1_cycles},
243	  \Cref{def:divisor_closed_point}). Both sides of the asserted
244	  equation are additive in $D$ (the right-hand side by linearity of
245	  $\deg$, \Cref{thm:divisor_degree_hom}; the left-hand side by the
246	  inductive step proved below), so it suffices to verify the formula
247	  for $D = 0$ and to show that it transports across the elementary
248	  modification $D \mapsto D + [P]$ at any closed point $P \in C$.
249	
250	  \emph{Base case $D = 0$.} The line bundle $\mathcal O_C(0)$ is the
251	  structure sheaf $\mathcal O_C$, and the formula
252	  $\chi(\mathcal O_C) = 1 - g$ reads
253	  \[
254	    \dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C)
255	    - \dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)
256	    \;=\; 1 - g.
257	  \]
258	  The second term is the definition of the genus,
259	  $\dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = g$ (\Cref{def:genus}). The
260	  first term is $\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C) = 1$: $C$ is a
261	  proper geometrically irreducible curve over the algebraically
262	  closed field $\bar k$, so its connected components agree with its
263	  geometrically connected components, and $H^0(C, \mathcal O_C)
264	  \cong \bar k$ as $\bar k$-vector spaces (the Hartshorne~I.3.4
265	  statement that ``$H^0(X, \mathcal O_X) = k$ for any projective
266	  variety'' over an algebraically closed field; the Mathlib home is
267	  the project's $H^0$-bridge in
268	  \texttt{Cohomology\_StructureSheafModuleK.tex}). Substituting these
269	  two values gives $1 - g = 1 - g$, as required.
270	
271	  \emph{Inductive step (Hartshorne's ``$D \leftrightsquigarrow D + P$''
272	  step).} Let $P \in C$ be a closed point. We follow Hartshorne's
273	  argument: view $P$ as a closed subscheme of $C$; its structure
274	  sheaf is the skyscraper sheaf $k(P) \cong \bar k$ supported at $P$,
275	  and its sheaf of ideals is $\mathcal O_C(-[P]) \subset \mathcal O_C$
276	  (the locally principal ideal sheaf cutting out $P$ in $C$). Hence
277	  the short exact sequence
278	  \[
279	    0 \to \mathcal O_C(-[P]) \to \mathcal O_C \to k(P) \to 0
280	  \]
281	  in $\mathbf{Coh}(C)$ (Hartshorne~II.6.18). Tensoring with the
282	  invertible sheaf $\mathcal O_C(D + [P])$ (a locally free
283	  $\mathcal O_C$-module of rank $1$, so tensoring preserves exactness
284	  and leaves the skyscraper sheaf $k(P)$ unchanged) yields
285	  \[
286	    0 \to \mathcal O_C(D)
287	    \to \mathcal O_C(D + [P])
288	    \to k(P) \to 0.
289	  \]
290	  Additivity of the Euler characteristic on a short exact sequence
291	  of coherent sheaves (Mathlib's preadditive-category Euler
292	  characteristic; see the Lean reference note at the end of this
293	  proof) gives
294	  \[
295	    \chi(\mathcal O_C(D + [P]))
296	    \;=\;
297	    \chi(\mathcal O_C(D)) + \chi(k(P)).
298	  \]
299	  The skyscraper sheaf $k(P)$ has
300	  $H^0(C, k(P)) \cong \bar k$ and $H^1(C, k(P)) = 0$ (the cohomology
301	  of a flasque sheaf supported at a closed point is concentrated in
302	  degree $0$, with $H^0$ equal to the stalk $\bar k$), so
303	  $\chi(k(P)) = 1$. Therefore
304	  \[
305	    \chi(\mathcal O_C(D + [P])) \;=\; \chi(\mathcal O_C(D)) + 1.
306	  \]
307	  On the other hand, $\deg([P]) = 1$ (every closed point contributes
308	  $1$ to the bare-sum degree of \Cref{def:divisor_degree}), so by
309	  \Cref{thm:divisor_degree_hom}
310	  \[
311	    \deg(D + [P]) + 1 - g \;=\; \deg(D) + 1 - g + 1.
312	  \]
313	  Comparing the two displayed identities, the formula
314	  $\chi(\mathcal O_C(D)) = \deg(D) + 1 - g$ holds for $D$ if and only
315	  if it holds for $D + [P]$. Since the closed-point divisors $[P]$
316	  generate $\Div(C)$ as a free abelian group, the formula propagates
317	  from $D = 0$ to every $D \in \Div(C)$ by induction on the (signed)
318	  number of generators in the expression of $D$.
319	
320	  \emph{Lean reference note on $\chi$-additivity.} Mathlib (snapshot
321	  \texttt{b80f227}) provides the Euler-characteristic API
322	  \texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Homology.EulerCharacteristic}
323	  (\texttt{HomologicalComplex.eulerChar}, \texttt{GradedObject.eulerChar})
324	  but does \emph{not} ship the specific additivity statement
325	  \texttt{CategoryTheory.ShortExact.eulerChar\_additive} [expected,
326	  not in Mathlib under that name]. The genus-$0$ proof of
327	  \Cref{thm:riemannRoch_genus_zero} therefore threads additivity of
328	  $\chi$ on a short exact sequence either as a project-side lemma
329	  proved inside \texttt{RRFormula.lean} (from
330	  \texttt{Submodule.finrank\_quotient\_add\_finrank} combined with
331	  the long exact sequence of $H^*$) or as a candidate Mathlib upstream
332	  PR. The base case $\chi(k(P)) = 1$ is closed by the Mathlib
333	  skyscraper-sheaf API together with the project's $H^0$-bridge
334	  (\Cref{def:Scheme_HModule}).
335	\end{proof}

337	\section{The Riemann--Roch formula in genus zero}
338	
339	\begin{theorem}
340	
341	
342	\leanok
343	[Riemann--Roch formula in genus zero]
344	  \label{thm:riemannRoch_genus_zero}
345	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.l_eq_degree_plus_one_of_genus_zero}
346	  \uses{def:l_invariant, def:divisor_degree, def:genus,
347	        thm:euler_char_eq_deg_plus_one_minus_genus,
348	        thm:principal_deg_zero}
349	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, Example~1.3.5 (genus-$0$ specialisation
350	  % of Riemann--Roch; the curve-is-rational consequence) (read from
351	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 314).
352	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Example 1.3.5. Recall that a curve is \emph{rational}
353	  % if it is birational to $\mathbf P^1$ (I, Ex. 6.1). Since curves in
354	  % this chapter are complete and nonsingular by definition, a curve
355	  % $X$ is rational if and only if $X \cong \mathbf P^1$ (I, 6.12). Now
356	  % using (1.3) we can show that $X$ is rational if and only if $g = 0$.
357	  % We already know that $p_a(\mathbf P^1) = 0$ (I, Ex. 7.2), so
358	  % conversely suppose given a curve $X$ of genus 0. Let $P, Q$ be two
359	  % distinct points of $X$ and apply Riemann--Roch to the divisor
360	  % $D = P - Q$. Since $\deg K - D = -2$, using (1.3.3) above, we have
361	  % $l(K - D) = 0$, and so we find $l(D) = 1$. But $D$ is a divisor of
362	  % degree 0, so by (1.2) we have $D \sim 0$, in other words $P \sim
363	  % Q$. But this implies $X$ is rational (II, 6.10.1)."
364	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, Example~1.3.5, p.~297.}
365	  Let $C$ be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
366	  $\bar k$ with $g(C) = 0$, and let $D \in \Div(C)$ with
367	  $\deg(D) \geq 0$. Then
368	  \[
369	    \ell(D) \;=\; \deg(D) + 1.
370	  \]
371	\end{theorem}
372	
373	\begin{proof}
374	  \uses{def:l_invariant, def:divisor_degree, def:genus,
375	        thm:euler_char_eq_deg_plus_one_minus_genus,
376	        thm:principal_deg_zero}
377	  Specialising
378	  \Cref{thm:euler_char_eq_deg_plus_one_minus_genus} to the genus-$0$
379	  hypothesis $g = 0$ yields
380	  \[
381	    \chi(\mathcal O_C(D)) \;=\; \deg(D) + 1.
382	  \]
383	  Unfolding $\chi$ via \Cref{def:eulerChar_curve} and the definition
384	  $\ell(D) = \dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(D))$
385	  (\Cref{def:l_invariant}), this rewrites as
386	  \[
387	    \ell(D) - \dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(D))
388	    \;=\; \deg(D) + 1.
389	  \]
390	  Under the standing genus-$0$ hypothesis and the assumption
391	  $\deg(D) \geq 0$, the higher-cohomology term vanishes:
392	  \[
393	    H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(D)) \;=\; 0
394	    \qquad \text{for } \deg(D) \geq 0.
395	  \]
396	  Substituting yields $\ell(D) = \deg(D) + 1$, as required.
397	
398	  \emph{The $H^1$-vanishing input.} On a smooth proper geometrically
399	  irreducible curve $C / \bar k$ with $g(C) = 0$, the vanishing
400	  $H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(D)) = 0$ for $\deg(D) \geq 0$ is the Hartshorne
401	  formulation Lemma~1.2 read covariantly: $\ell(D) \neq 0$ forces
402	  $\deg(D) \geq 0$; equivalently, for a curve where every
403	  degree-$0$-or-higher invertible sheaf is generated by global
404	  sections after a single point-twist, $H^1$ of the twist must vanish
405	  by the long exact sequence of the skyscraper extension at any
406	  closed point. In the chain of sub-build chapters, this $H^1$
407	  vanishing is the natural content of \texttt{RR.3} (sibling chapter
408	  \texttt{RiemannRoch\_OcOfD.tex}, to be added), where the line
409	  bundle $\mathcal O_C(D)$ and its cohomology are constructed; once
410	  \texttt{RR.3} is closed, the present theorem inherits the
411	  vanishing as an instance, and the proof closes verbatim as
412	  displayed. Pending \texttt{RR.3}, the Lean signature
413	  \texttt{l\_eq\_degree\_plus\_one\_of\_genus\_zero} threads the
414	  $H^1$-vanishing hypothesis explicitly as a named premise; the
415	  iteration that closes \texttt{RR.3} then drops the premise in a
416	  follow-up signature simplification.
417	
418	  \emph{Sub-build note for \texttt{RR.4}.} The downstream consumer of
419	  this theorem is the genus-$0$ classification
420	  \Cref{prop:genusZero_curve_iso_P1} of
421	  \texttt{AbelianVarietyRigidity.tex}: with $\ell(D) = \deg(D) + 1$
422	  and the degree-zero principal-divisor identity
423	  (\Cref{thm:principal_deg_zero}), Hartshorne's Example~1.3.5 chain
424	  produces a degree-$1$ morphism $C \to \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$ via
425	  \texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections} applied to the two-dimensional
426	  $H^0(C, \mathcal O_C([P]))$, which is then upgraded to an
427	  isomorphism in \texttt{RR.4}.
428	\end{proof}

430	\section{Out of scope}
431	
432	The following items are deliberately \emph{not} part of \texttt{RR.2}
433	and are sequenced into the sibling sub-build chapters:
434	
435	\begin{itemize}
436	  \item The general-$g$ Riemann--Roch theorem
437	    $\ell(D) - \ell(K - D) = \deg(D) + 1 - g$ (Hartshorne~IV.1.3 in
438	    its full form). This requires the canonical divisor $K$, the
439	    canonical sheaf $\Omega^1_{C / \bar k} = \omega_C$, and
440	    Serre duality $H^1(C, \mathcal L) \cong H^0(C, \omega_C \otimes
441	    \mathcal L^{-1})^*$. Mathlib has none of these for schemes, and
442	    the genus-$0$ critical path does not require them; the general
443	    statement is queued for a follow-up chapter (post-RR-bridge).
444	  \item The construction of the invertible sheaf $\mathcal O_C(D)$
445	    from a Weil divisor $D$, the linear-equivalence isomorphism
446	    $\mathcal O_C(D) \cong \mathcal O_C(D')$ when $D \sim D'$, and
447	    the computation of $H^0(C, \mathcal O_C([P])) \cong \bar k \cdot
448	    1 \oplus \bar k \cdot t$ (\emph{a non-constant rational function
449	    $t$ exists}) live in \texttt{RR.3} (sibling chapter
450	    \texttt{RiemannRoch\_OcOfD.tex}, to be added). Inside the proof
451	    of \Cref{thm:riemannRoch_genus_zero} the dependency on
452	    $\mathcal O_C(D)$ is reduced to its $H^0$ functor and the short
453	    exact sequence of the closed point.
454	  \item The vanishing $H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(D)) = 0$ for
455	    $\deg(D) \geq 0$ on a genus-$0$ curve is consumed as a named
456	    premise by \Cref{thm:riemannRoch_genus_zero}; its closure is
457	    part of \texttt{RR.3} (where the cohomology of
458	    $\mathcal O_{\mathbb P^1}(d)$ is computed) and is recovered for
459	    a general smooth proper genus-$0$ curve via the isomorphism
460	    $C \cong \mathbb P^1$ of \texttt{RR.4} or directly via the
461	    base-change argument inside \texttt{RR.3}.
462	  \item The classification ``a smooth proper geometrically irreducible
463	    curve over $\bar k$ with $g = 0$ is isomorphic to
464	    $\mathbb P^1$'' (Hartshorne Example~IV.1.3.5 proper) is the
465	    headline of \texttt{RR.4} (sibling chapter
466	    \texttt{RiemannRoch\_RationalIsoP1.tex}, to be added). The
467	    present chapter supplies one of its two inputs (the $\ell(D) =
468	    \deg(D) + 1$ identity); the other input is the degree-$0$
469	    principal-divisor identity \Cref{thm:principal_deg_zero} from
470	    \texttt{RR.1}.
471	  \item Higher-cohomology bookkeeping ($\chi$ as an alternating sum
472	    over all $i \geq 0$, the Grothendieck vanishing
473	    $H^i(C, \mathcal F) = 0$ for $i > \dim C = 1$) is not separately
474	    formalised here; the chapter consumes only the two-term form
475	    $\chi(\mathcal F) = \dim H^0 - \dim H^1$ which is the standing
476	    curve specialisation.
477	\end{itemize}
